questions.q: What is the 'Agni Pariksha' a trial by?
questions.explanation: Agni Pariksha literally means the 'trial by fire,' from the Sanskrit word Agni meaning fire.

questions.q: Which woman underwent the Agni Pariksha in the Ramayana?
questions.explanation: Sita, the wife of Rama, entered the fire to prove her purity after her captivity.

questions.q: Whose wife was Sita when she underwent the trial by fire?
questions.explanation: Sita was the devoted wife of Rama, prince of Ayodhya and avatar of Vishnu.

questions.q: Which god bore Sita out of the fire unharmed?
questions.explanation: Agni, the god of fire, emerged from the flames and presented Sita unharmed, affirming her virtue.

questions.q: The Agni Pariksha took place after Sita's release from whose captivity?
questions.explanation: Sita had been held captive by the demon-king Ravana in Lanka before her rescue.

questions.q: In which kingdom did the Agni Pariksha occur, just after the great war?
questions.explanation: The trial took place in Lanka, immediately after Rama's victory over Ravana.

questions.q: What was the Agni Pariksha meant to prove about Sita?
questions.explanation: The ordeal was intended to demonstrate Sita's untainted purity and fidelity after her long captivity.

questions.q: What outcome did the fire reveal about Sita?
questions.explanation: The fire did not harm Sita, proving her purity and innocence to all who doubted her.

questions.q: Rama's victory over Ravana was won in a war fought in which land?
questions.explanation: The decisive war against Ravana was fought in Lanka, where Sita had been imprisoned.

questions.q: The Agni Pariksha episode appears in which epic?
questions.explanation: The trial by fire is a celebrated episode in the Ramayana, attributed to the sage Valmiki.

questions.q: In which kanda (book) of Valmiki's Ramayana does the Agni Pariksha primarily occur?
questions.explanation: The trial by fire occurs in the Yuddha Kanda, the book of war, after Ravana's defeat.

questions.q: Who built the fire (pyre) into which Sita entered, at her own request?
questions.explanation: At Sita's request, Lakshmana prepared the blazing pyre, though he did so with great reluctance.

questions.q: What feeling did Rama display toward Sita before the trial, prompting the ordeal?
questions.explanation: Rama spoke harshly and expressed doubt about Sita's purity after her time in Ravana's house, which moved her to seek the fire.

questions.q: How did Sita feel when Rama spoke harsh words doubting her, before the ordeal?
questions.explanation: Sita was deeply hurt and grieved by Rama's harsh words, and chose the fire to vindicate her honor.

questions.q: Who circumambulated Rama before entering the flames, as a final act of reverence?
questions.explanation: Sita reverently circled Rama and prayed before stepping into the fire to prove her innocence.

questions.q: To whom did Sita appeal as witness to her purity just before entering the fire?
questions.explanation: Sita called upon Agni himself to be the witness and judge of her unstained virtue as she entered the flames.

questions.q: When Agni delivered Sita from the fire, in what condition were her garments and garlands?
questions.explanation: Agni presented Sita with her garments, garlands, and ornaments completely unscorched, proving the fire had not touched her.

questions.q: What did Agni declare to Rama as he restored Sita to him?
options: That Sita was sinless and pure
questions.explanation: Agni proclaimed that Sita was wholly free of sin and faultless, urging Rama to take her back without hesitation.

questions.q: After the Agni Pariksha, what did Rama say had been his deeper purpose in testing Sita publicly?
options: To prove her purity to the world
questions.explanation: Rama explained that he never truly doubted Sita, but needed the public ordeal so the whole world would accept her purity.

questions.q: Which monkey ally had earlier found Sita in captivity before this episode in Lanka?
questions.explanation: Hanuman had discovered Sita in the Ashoka grove of Lanka during his earlier reconnaissance mission.

questions.q: In which garden was Sita kept captive in Lanka before her rescue and the trial?
questions.explanation: Ravana held Sita captive in the Ashoka Vatika, a grove of ashoka trees in Lanka.

questions.q: Besides Agni, which gods are described as appearing or assembling at the trial in the Yuddha Kanda?
options: A host of gods including Brahma and Indra
questions.explanation: A great assembly of gods, including Brahma and Indra, gathered at the scene of the trial in the Yuddha Kanda.

questions.q: During the assembly at the trial, the gods remind Rama of his true divine identity as which deity?
questions.explanation: At the trial the assembled gods revealed to Rama that he was Vishnu (Narayana) incarnate, sent to slay Ravana.

questions.q: Who is the divine sage-father that appears and addresses Rama during this episode in the Yuddha Kanda?
questions.explanation: Brahma, the creator, appears among the gods and reveals Rama's divine nature as Vishnu during the episode.

questions.q: In a famous later interpretation tied to Sita's purity, some traditions hold that the real Sita had been hidden and replaced by which figure before her abduction?
questions.explanation: Later traditions such as the Adhyatma Ramayana hold that a shadow or illusory Sita (Maya Sita) was abducted, the true Sita being kept hidden in fire until the Agni Pariksha restored her.

questions.q: According to the shadow-Sita tradition, into whose keeping was the true Sita entrusted before the abduction, only to be returned at the fire trial?
questions.explanation: In the Maya Sita tradition, the real Sita was entrusted to Agni and concealed in fire, then returned to Rama at the Agni Pariksha.

questions.q: Which Lankan ally of Rama, Ravana's own brother, was present at the victory that led to the trial?
questions.explanation: Vibhishana, Ravana's righteous brother, defected to Rama and was present at the victory preceding the trial.

questions.q: The Agni Pariksha in the Yuddha Kanda is distinct from a later, second rejection of Sita that occurs in which book of the Ramayana?
questions.explanation: A second banishment of Sita over public rumor occurs in the Uttara Kanda, separate from the fire trial of the Yuddha Kanda.

questions.q: What is the name of Sita's father, the king whose daughter underwent the fire ordeal?
questions.explanation: Sita was the daughter of King Janaka of Mithila, which is why she is also called Janaki.

questions.q: What epithet of Sita, referring to her birth from the earth's furrow, underscores the unjustness of doubting her purity at the fire trial?
questions.explanation: Sita is called Ayonija, born not from a womb but from the earth's furrow when Janaka plowed the field, marking her divine and pure origin.
